---
title: "12 · Shapes and Angles"
subtitle: "Lines that meet, corners that turn — the alphabet of geometry"
lesson-id: "t1.shapes-angles"
tier: 1
prerequisites: []
unlocks: ["t1.area-perimeter-volume"]
applied-domains: ["game-dev", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 30
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

This lesson stands alone — it has no maths prerequisites in this
curriculum. You only need:

1. To know what a **straight line** looks like (a taut string, the edge
   of a ruler).
2. The willingness to think about *shape* and *direction* rather than
   numbers for a while.
3. (Optional) Pencil and paper to sketch.
:::

## Touch

Drag the slider to set the **angle** between two lines meeting at a
point. Watch the corner open and close.

```{ojs}
viewof angleDeg = Inputs.range([0, 180], {value: 60, step: 5, label: "Angle (degrees):"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const W = 480, H = 280, cx = W/2, cy = H - 40, len = 180;
  const a = angleDeg * Math.PI / 180;
  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("viewBox", `0 0 ${W} ${H}`)
    .attr("style", "width:100%; max-width:520px; display:block; margin: 0 auto;");

  // Reference line (horizontal right)
  const x1 = cx + len, y1 = cy;
  // Rotating line
  const x2 = cx + len * Math.cos(-a), y2 = cy + len * Math.sin(-a);

  svg.append("line").attr("x1", cx).attr("y1", cy).attr("x2", x1).attr("y2", y1)
    .attr("stroke", "#888").attr("stroke-width", 2);
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", cx).attr("y1", cy).attr("x2", x2).attr("y2", y2)
    .attr("stroke", "#2c5f7c").attr("stroke-width", 2.5);

  // Arc showing the angle
  const arcR = 50;
  const arcEndX = cx + arcR * Math.cos(-a), arcEndY = cy + arcR * Math.sin(-a);
  const large = a > Math.PI ? 1 : 0;
  svg.append("path")
    .attr("d", `M ${cx + arcR},${cy} A ${arcR},${arcR} 0 ${large} 0 ${arcEndX},${arcEndY}`)
    .attr("fill", "none").attr("stroke", "#c2185b").attr("stroke-width", 2);
  // Angle label
  const labA = -a / 2;
  svg.append("text")
    .attr("x", cx + (arcR + 20) * Math.cos(labA))
    .attr("y", cy + (arcR + 20) * Math.sin(labA))
    .attr("font-size", "16").attr("fill", "#c2185b").attr("font-weight", "bold")
    .text(`${angleDeg}°`);
  // Vertex
  svg.append("circle").attr("cx", cx).attr("cy", cy).attr("r", 4).attr("fill", "#1f2933");

  // Name annotations
  let name;
  if (angleDeg === 0) name = "(zero — lines coincide)";
  else if (angleDeg < 90) name = "acute (less than 90°)";
  else if (angleDeg === 90) name = "right (exactly 90°)";
  else if (angleDeg < 180) name = "obtuse (between 90° and 180°)";
  else name = "straight (exactly 180°)";

  svg.append("text").attr("x", cx).attr("y", 28).attr("text-anchor", "middle")
    .attr("font-size", "16").attr("fill", "#1f2933").attr("font-weight", "bold")
    .text(name);

  return svg.node();
}
```

An **angle** measures *how much one line turns away from another*, in
**degrees**. A full turn (back to the start) is 360°. A half-turn is
180°. A quarter-turn is **90°** — the **right angle**.

## Wonder

1. Open and close the angle. Is the *length* of the lines part of the
   angle? Or only the spread between them?
2. What angles do you see in the corners of a book, a window, a phone
   screen?
3. A triangle has three corners. If you measured the angles at each
   corner and added them up, do you think the answer would always be the
   same?

## Discover

### Problem 1

Sketch (or imagine) these shapes and name them:

(a) Three straight sides.
(b) Four straight sides, all the same length, all corners right-angled.
(c) Four straight sides, opposite sides equal, all corners right-angled.
(d) A flat curve where every point is the same distance from one fixed
    centre.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
(a) **Triangle.** (b) **Square.** (c) **Rectangle.** (d) **Circle.**

Notice the structure: shapes are described by *number of sides*, *side
lengths*, and *angles*. These three pieces of data determine almost
every elementary shape.
</details>

### Problem 2

Sketch a triangle. Cut it out (or just imagine cutting it). Tear off the
three corners. Place them next to each other along a straight line.

What do you find?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
A straight line corresponds to an angle of 180°.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**The three corners exactly fill a straight line — i.e. they sum to 180°.**

This is the most famous fact in elementary geometry:

> **The three angles of any triangle sum to 180°.**

True for *any* triangle — pointy, flat, equilateral, scalene. The
classroom experiment with torn corners is a *physical proof*. Tier 3
will give a careful proof using parallel lines.
</details>

### Problem 3

A triangle has angles **40°** and **75°**. What is the third angle?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Use the rule from *P2*: total = 180°.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$180 - 40 - 75 = 65°$. **65°.** Algebra (Lesson 11) makes the calculation
trivial: if the third angle is $x$, then $40 + 75 + x = 180$, so
$x = 65$.
</details>

### Problem 4

Imagine walking around the boundary of a square. Each corner, you turn
by 90° (a right angle). After four corners, you've turned through how
much in total — and back to your starting direction?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Four right angles, each 90°. Sum?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$4 \times 90° = 360°$ — a **full turn**. After walking around any
*convex* shape, you turn through 360° in total.

This generalises beautifully: a triangle's *exterior angles* (the turn
at each corner if you walked along it) also sum to 360°. So do a
pentagon's, a hexagon's, and any convex shape. The interior angles add
up differently for each shape, but the *exterior* angles always sum to
360°. Geometry is full of such conserved totals.
</details>

### Problem 5

Two lines meet, forming an angle of **53°** on one side. What is the
angle on the *other* side of the same line?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The two angles together fill a straight line.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$180 - 53 = 127°$. **127°.** Two angles forming a straight line are
called **supplementary**, and they sum to 180° by definition. Two angles
forming a right angle are called **complementary** and sum to 90°.

The two angles on opposite sides of a single straight line are called
**vertical angles** when two lines cross — and an important Tier 3 fact
is that they are *always equal*.
</details>

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

A **point** has position but no size. A **line** is the path traced by
moving in one fixed direction. A **line segment** is a finite piece of a
line, with two endpoints.

An **angle** measures the turn between two lines (or segments) that meet
at a common point — the **vertex**. We measure angles in **degrees**: a
full turn is **360°**, a half-turn 180°, a quarter-turn **90°** (a
**right angle**).

Special angle names:

- **Acute:** less than 90°.
- **Right:** exactly 90°.
- **Obtuse:** between 90° and 180°.
- **Straight:** exactly 180°.

A **polygon** is a closed shape with straight sides. Common ones:

- **Triangle** (3 sides), **quadrilateral** (4), **pentagon** (5),
  **hexagon** (6), ...
- A **square** has 4 equal sides and 4 right angles.
- A **rectangle** has opposite sides equal and 4 right angles.
- A **regular** polygon has all sides equal and all angles equal.

A **circle** is the set of points at a fixed **radius** from a centre.

**The triangle angle-sum:** the three interior angles of any triangle
sum to **180°**.

:::

> *Two lines meeting — the angle between them;*
> *Three lines closing — a triangle, whose corners always sum to 180°;*
> *A walk around a closed shape returns you 360° to the start.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> A triangle has two equal sides and a vertex angle (between the equal
> sides) of **50°**. Find the other two angles.

A competent solver uses a small fact about isoceles triangles:

> **In an isoceles triangle, the angles opposite the two equal sides are
> equal.**

So the other two angles are equal — call each $x$. Sum to 180°:
$$50 + x + x = 180 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 2x = 130 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x = 65°.$$

**Answer:** the other two angles are **65° each**. Verify: $50 + 65 + 65
= 180°$ ✓.

The "equal sides imply equal opposite angles" fact will be proved
properly in Tier 2 / Tier 3 geometry.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Classify each: 30°, 90°, 130°, 180°.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
acute, right, obtuse, straight.
</details>

**W2.** A triangle has two angles of 50° and 70°. Find the third.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$180 - 50 - 70 = 60°$.
</details>

**W3.** A square's four angles are all equal and sum to... ? Hence each
is... ?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Sum $= 360°$ (the four corners). Each $= 90°$ — confirming squares are
right-angled.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** A triangle has all three angles equal. What is each angle?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Three equal angles summing to 180°. So each is...
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$180 \div 3 = 60°$. This is the **equilateral** triangle: all three
sides equal, all three angles equal to 60°. The most symmetric triangle.
</details>

**P2.** A regular pentagon has 5 equal interior angles. The interior
angles of *any* pentagon sum to $(5 - 2) \times 180° = 540°$. What is
each interior angle of a regular pentagon?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Total ÷ 5.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$540 \div 5 = 108°$. (Each angle of a regular pentagon is 108°.)

The general formula for the interior-angle sum of a polygon with $n$
sides is $(n - 2) \times 180°$. You can derive it by noticing that any
$n$-sided polygon can be sliced into $(n - 2)$ triangles from a single
vertex.
</details>

**P3.** Two angles of a triangle are equal, and the third is *twice*
each of them. Find all three angles.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Let each equal angle be $x$. Then the third is $2x$. Use Lesson 11.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x + x + 2x = 180$, so $4x = 180$ and $x = 45°$. The angles are 45°, 45°,
90°. **A right-angled isoceles triangle.** This shape is the diagonal of
a square — a recurring shape in geometry.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** What angle do the hands of a clock make at exactly **3:00**?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The clock face is a circle (360°) divided into 12 equal segments.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
At 3:00, the minute hand is at 12 and the hour hand is at 3. They are
3 hours apart, which is $\tfrac{3}{12} = \tfrac{1}{4}$ of the full
circle. $\tfrac{1}{4} \times 360 = 90°$.

**90° — a right angle.** That's why a clock at 3:00 (or 9:00) makes a
visually familiar L-shape.
</details>

**S2.** A 3D triangle in a video game is described by three points
(vertices). The game engine needs to compute the angles between pairs
of edges to do *lighting* (which way is the surface facing?). The
**angle between two vectors** $\vec{u}$ and $\vec{v}$ is given by
$$\cos\theta = \frac{\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v}}{|\vec{u}|\,|\vec{v}|}.$$
You don't need to compute this now — but recognise: even *3D shading*
is a question about angles. What does this say about the importance of
angles?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Every modern game renders 3D scenes by decomposing surfaces into
millions of tiny triangles, and computing the angle between each
triangle's surface direction and the direction of the light. The result
controls the brightness of each pixel.

The simple "three corners of a triangle add to 180°" you met today
generalises into the **trigonometry** of Tier 3 and the **vector
algebra** of Tier 5. From a child's protractor to real-time 3D graphics,
the same idea — *angle between two directions* — is the through line.

Angles are *not* a small topic. They are one of the most reused ideas
in all of applied mathematics.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Bigger lines mean a bigger angle."** No — angle measures only the
   *turn* between the lines, not their length. Two short pencils at a
   wide angle, and two long swords at a narrow angle, can have very
   different angles regardless of length.
2. **"All four-sided shapes are squares or rectangles."** No — there
   are also rhombuses (equal sides, no right angles), parallelograms,
   trapeziums, and irregular quadrilaterals.
3. **"360° is special — it's the only full turn."** It's the
   *convention* for full turn (chosen by the Babylonians for their
   base-60 number system). Mathematicians often use **radians** instead
   (a full turn is $2\pi$), which we'll meet in Tier 3.
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you tear the corners off a paper triangle and convince a friend
   that they sum to 180°?
2. Without scrolling back, can you find the third angle of a triangle
   given two of them are 35° and 95°?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

(Answer: $180 - 35 - 95 = 50°$.)

- **3 yes:** ready for **[Lesson 13 — Area, Perimeter, Volume](../13-area-perimeter-volume/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **P2** — the polygon angle-sum formula is one of
  geometry's gems.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P2 and P4*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

None within this curriculum. Geometry has its own logical foundation —
this lesson sits at the start of the geometric strand.

### What this unlocks

- **[Area, Perimeter, Volume](../13-area-perimeter-volume/index.qmd)** —
  with shapes and angles named, we can measure them.
- **Tier 3 — Trigonometry** — the *ratios* of triangle sides as
  functions of the angles. Every angle calculation in physics or
  graphics ultimately uses trigonometry.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development & graphics.** Every 3D model is a mesh of triangles.
Every lighting calculation is an angle calculation. The angle between
the camera and a surface determines what you see. *Stretch S2* gives
the high-level view.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Mathematical research.** Geometry is one of mathematics' two oldest
branches (with arithmetic). Euclid's *Elements* (c. 300 BCE) systematised
plane geometry from a few axioms — its method (axiom → theorem → proof)
is the model for all subsequent rigorous mathematics. Kiselev's
*Geometry* (Russia, 19th century) is the modern reincarnation.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Everywhere else.** Architecture, navigation (compass bearings),
astronomy (positions of stars), surveying, engineering, art and design,
sports (camera angles in cricket replays), and traffic engineering
(intersection angles) all depend on the angle vocabulary.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones honoured:** Pestalozzi (the angle widget makes turning
  literal), Boole (the *do the angles depend on the lengths?* question
  surfaces a key intuition), Russian school (the corner-tearing
  experiment is a physical *proof*; Kiselev's geometry texts use this
  exact demonstration), Bhāskara (single-box Name + verse), Euclid (the
  whole geometric vocabulary traces to *Elements*).
- **Self-study guarantees** all present.
:::
